coredata.dc:title: Fluorinated graphene quantum dots-induced defect passivation of perovskite film toward stable and efficient perovskite solar cell
coredata.prism:copyright: © 2025 Hydrogen Energy Publications LLC. Published by Elsevier Ltd. All rights are reserved, including those for text and data mining, AI training, and similar technologies.
coredata.prism:publisher: Hydrogen Energy Publications LLC. Published by Elsevier Ltd. All rights are reserved, including those for text and data mining, AI training, and similar technologies.
coredata.dc:creator: Chen, Yao
Chen, Jiajun
Wang, Xiaorui
Deng, Penglin
Shen, Yijun
Wang, Xiaohong
coredata.dc:description: Passivation engineering has been considered as an effective strategy to eliminate defects at grain boundaries and interface of perovskite films. Herein, fluorinated graphene quantum dots (FGQDs), as an antisolvent additive, are successfully introduced into perovskite films for defect passivating. FGQDs can regulate the crystallization process of perovskite via chemical bonding with the undercoordinated Pb2+. As a result, the high-quality perovskite film with enlarged grain size and reduced defect density can be obtained. FGQDs modified Cs0.05MA0.16FA0.79Pb(I0.83Br0.17)3 device yields a high-power conversion efficiency of 21.74 %, and a higher PCE of 24.12 % is delivered for the device assembled with FA-based perovskite with a composition of Cs0.05(FA0.98MA0.02)0.95Pb(I0.98Br0.02)3. Due to the excellent hydrophobicity of F atoms, the unencapsulated devices exhibit good long-term stability under 50 % relative humidity condition (85 % efficiency retention after 30 days). Our findings provide a new passivation strategy toward stable and efficient perovskite solar cells.
coredata.dcterms:subject: Fluorinated graphene quantum dots
Antisolvent additive
Defect passivation
Humidity stability
Hole extraction
Organic-inorganic hybrid perovskite solar cells

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: (a) TEM image of FGQDs with the inset showing the HR-TEM image. (b) Size distributions of FGQDs. (c) AFM image and the corresponding (d) diameter distributions of the selected FGQDs.
(a) XPS spectra (b) Raman spectra of FG and FGQDs.

SEM images of (a) pristine perovskite film and (b) FGQDs-treated perovskite film. Statistical histograms of the particle size distribution from TEM images of (c) pristine perovskite film and (d) FGQDs-treated perovskite film. In situ PL spectra of (e) wet pristine perovskite film and (f) wet FGQDs-treated perovskite film during spin coating.
(a) High-resolution Pb 4f XPS spectra of the perovskite films with and without FGQDs. (b) High-resolution F 1s XPS spectra of FGQDs and FGQDs + PVK.

(a) Steady-state PL spectra and (b) TRPL spectra of Glass/Perovskite/spiro-OMeTAD and Glass/Perovskite + FGQDs/spiro-OMeTAD. (c) Nyquist plots of devices with and without FGQDs treatment. The inset of Fig. 5c shows the equivalent circuit mode.

(a) Typical J-V curves of control and target devices under forward and reverse scan directions. (b) Typical J-V curves of FA-based devices with and without FGQDs under reverse scan direction. (c) Stable J
sc and PCE of control and target devices under MPP tracking conditions. (d) Humidity stability test of the unencapsulated control and target devices at ∼50 % RH and 25 °C in the dark. The insets show water contact angles on the surface of control and target perovskite films.

head.ce:title: Fluorinated graphene quantum dots-induced defect passivation of perovskite film toward stable and efficient perovskite solar cell

ce:affiliation.ce:label: a
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: School of Chemistry and Chemical Engineering, Hainan University, Haikou, 570228, China

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: School of Chemistry and Chemical Engineering

ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: School of Chemistry and Chemical Engineering, Hainan University, Haikou 570228, China

ce:affiliation.ce:label: b
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: School of Marine Science and Engineering, State Key Laboratory of Marine Resource Utilization in South China Sea, Hainan University, Haikou, 570228, China

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: School of Marine Science and Engineering
State Key Laboratory of Marine Resource Utilization in South China Sea

ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: School of Marine Science and Engineering, State Key Laboratory of Marine Resource Utilization in South China Sea, Hainan University, Haikou 570228, PR China

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: Passivation engineering has been considered as an effective strategy to eliminate defects at grain boundaries and interface of perovskite films. Herein, fluorinated graphene quantum dots (FGQDs), as an antisolvent additive, are successfully introduced into perovskite films for defect passivating. FGQDs can regulate the crystallization process of perovskite via chemical bonding with the undercoordinated Pb2+. As a result, the high-quality perovskite film with enlarged grain size and reduced defect density can be obtained. FGQDs modified Cs0.05MA0.16FA0.79Pb(I0.83Br0.17)3 device yields a high-power conversion efficiency of 21.74 %, and a higher PCE of 24.12 % is delivered for the device assembled with FA-based perovskite with a composition of Cs0.05(FA0.98MA0.02)0.95Pb(I0.98Br0.02)3. Due to the excellent hydrophobicity of F atoms, the unencapsulated devices exhibit good long-term stability under 50 % relative humidity condition (85 % efficiency retention after 30 days). Our findings provide a new passivation strategy toward stable and efficient perovskite solar cells.

ce:section.ce:para: Organic-inorganic lead halide perovskites have attracted high attention from the materials research community and are driving the development of low cost and clean energy solutions to achieve sustainable energy [1–5]. In recent years, the power conversion efficiency (PCE) of small-area perovskite solar cells (PSCs) has soared from the initial 3.9 %–27.0 % [6,7]. However, perovskite thin films prepared by low temperature synthesis process inevitably bring a large number of defects at the interface and grain boundaries (GBs), such as dislocation, gap, anion vacancy and coordinated unsaturated lead cations [8]. As the recombination center of carriers, however, these defects greatly reduce the photovoltaic performance of PSCs [9]. In addition, the presence of these defects accelerates the infiltration of water from environment, and thus degrades perovskite films, leading to the poor ambient stability of PSCs [10]. At present, the passivation of defects in perovskite films has been viewed as the most effective strategy for the preparation of efficient and stable PSCs [11]. Wang et al. reported a coordinated passivation strategy utilizing an organic additive 6-methoxynicotinamide hydrochloride (APO) bearing multiple functional groups, which was introduced in perovskite films to improve the carrier dynamics of perovskite films under low-temperature and low-light dual extreme conditions [12]. Deng et al. proposed a targeted GB passivation strategy through solvent effects by incorporating symmetrical biphenyl molecules, aiming to mitigate defects at GBs and optimize energy level arrangements through their electric dipole effects [13]. Zhou et al. explored the multiple effects of benzylamine 1to passivate perovskite compositions, which enabled the rational design of molecular strategies to improve the material quality and device performance of metal-halide perovskites [14].

Graphene quantum dots (GQDs) not only have the excellent characteristics of traditional semiconductor quantum dots, such as adjustable excitation/emission wavelength, strong light luminescence and good electrochemical performance [15], but also can effectively overcome the defects of traditional semiconductor quantum dots (e.g. high toxicity, poor biocompatibility) [16]. Moreover, GQDs possess the advantages of a wide range of sources, easy synthesis and functionalization [17]. Therefore, GQD has been considered as an ideal substitute material for traditional semiconductor quantum dots [18]. Up to now, a large number of studies have shown that GQDs have great application value in the fields of biology, medicine, chemical industry and electronics, including chemical sensing, biosensing, biological imaging, drug delivery, photodynamic therapy, photocatalysis, electrocatalysis, etc. [19]. Especially, GQDs show enormous potential in PSCs due to their excellent physical and chemical properties. Gao et al. synthesized imidazole bromide functionalized GQDs for defect passivation of the interface between the electron transport layer (ETL) and formamidinium lead iodide (FAPbI3) perovskite layer, thereby leading to a better energy level alignment and improved FAPbI3 film quality [20]. Furthermore, Cai also applied imidazole-modified GQDs to improve light utilization through down-conversion [21]. Han et al. synthesized and characterized the chiroptic behavior and doping effects of both chiral and racemic GQDs. Compared with racemic GQDs, the chiral specificity of chiral GQDs leads to reduced nonradiative recombination and enhanced charge transport efficiency [22].

In this work, the fluorine atoms, with great advantages of high electronegativity and good hydrophobicity, are combined with GQDs to prepare fluorinated graphene quantum dots (FGQDs), which are further introduced into perovskite thin films as an additive of antisolvent. It is interestingly found that the quality of perovskite thin films can be greatly improved via Lewis acid-base interaction. Benefitted from efficient defect-passivation and improved charge extraction at perovskite/hole transport layer (HTL), the resultant devices assembled with Cs0.05MA0.16FA0.79Pb(I0.83Br0.17)3 perovskites yield a champion PCE of 21.74 %, along with substantially improved humidity stability. A higher efficiency of 24.12 % can be delivered for the FA-based device. Hence, this passivation strategy can be easily extended to other perovskite-based defect-materials for advanced PSCs.

All the chemicals and solvents were purchased from commercial sources and used as received without further purification unless stated otherwise. SnO2 colloid precursor (15 wt%) was obtained from Alfa Aesar (tin (IV) oxide). N,N-dimethylformamide (DMF), isopropanol (IPA), acetonitrile, dimethyl sulphoxide (DMSO), chlorobenzene (CB), lead iodide (PbI2), lead bromide (PbBr2) and cesium iodide(CsI) were purchased from Sigma-Aldrich. 4-tert-butylpyridine (t-BP) and lithium bis(trifluoromethanesulphonyl)imide (Li-TFSI) were purchased from Xi'an Polymer Light Technology Corp. Formamidinium iodide (FAI), methylammonium and bromide (MABr) were purchased from GreatCell Solar. 2,2',7,7'-Tetrakis(N,N-di-p-methoxyphenylamino)-9,9'-spirobifluorene (Spiro-OMeTAD) was purchased from Borun New Materials Co. Ltd, China. Fluorinated graphite (FG, Grade II) was purchased from Shanghai CarFluor Ltd. and used as provided. Sulfuric acid (H2SO4) and nitric acid (HNO3) were purchased from XiLong Science Co. Ltd, China.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Preparation of fluorinated graphene quantum dots
ce:section.ce:para: 50 mg of FG was first dissolved in mixed solution consisting of 10 mL concentrated H2SO4 and 3 mL ultra-pure water, and then the mixture was sonicated for 1.5 h to achieve a homogeneous dispersion. Subsequently, 10 mL concentrated H2SO4 and 60 mL HNO3 were added, the mixture was sonicated for 3 h, followed by heating at 70 °C for 24 h. After cooling down to room temperature, 200 mL ultra-pure water was added and the pH of the solution was adjusted to neutral. Upon vacuum filtration with 0.22 mm filter, FGQDs were finally collected by dialysis (molecular weight: 500 Da).

The Cs0.05MA0.16FA0.79Pb(I0.83Br0.17)3 perovskite precursor solution was prepared by dissolving PbI2 (548.6 mg), PbBr2 (77.07 mg), FAI (190.12 mg) and MABr (21.84 mg) in mixed solvent containing DMF and DMSO (4:1 v/v), followed by the addition of 34 μL CsI stock solution (2 M in DMSO). The spin-coating procedure was performed at 2000 rpm for 10 s, followed by 6000 rpm for 45 s. At 15 s before the last spin-coating step, chlorobenzene anti-solvent (150 μL) was dripped onto the substrate, followed by heating at 110 °C for 60 min.

For the preparation of FA-based perovskite films with a composition of Cs0.05 (FA0.98MA0.02)0.95Pb(I0.98Br0.02)3, 1.65 M perovskite precursor solution was prepared by dissolving CsI, FAI, MABr, PbBr2 and PbI2 in 1 ml mixed solvent of DMF and DMSO at a volume ratio of 4:1. Additional 7 mol% PbI2 and 15 mol% MACl was added to the precursor solution and the solution was stirred overnight. The filtered perovskite precursor was then spin-coated on substrate at 1,000 rpm for 10 s and 5,000 rpm for 40 s in a nitrogen glovebox. During the second spinning step, 250 μl of CB anti-solvent was dropped quickly at 25 s. Afterwards, the film was annealed at 110 °C for 20 min.

Fluorine-doped tin oxide (FTO) glasses were cleaned by sequentially washing with detergent, deionized (DI) water, acetone and IPA, and then treated with UV-ozone for 20 min before use. A thin layer of SnO2 nanoparticle film was prepared by spin-coating SnO2 precursor (SnO2: deionized water = 1:3, v/v) onto the as-cleaned FTO substrate at 4000 rpm for 30 s, and subsequently annealed in ambient air at 150 °C for 30 min. The perovskite film was spin-coated on FTO/SnO2 substrate by the abovementioned method. For the fabrication of the target perovskite film, a certain amount of FGQDs (2-5 mg/mL) was added into CB anti-solvent. Thereafter, the hole transport layer was grown on the perovskite film by spin-coating Spiro-OMeTAD precursor solution, consisting of 72.3 mg Spiro-OMeTAD, 35 μL Li-TFSI stock solution (260 mg Li-TFSI in 1 mL acetonitrile), 30 μL 4-tertbutylpyridine (t-BP) and 1 mL CB, at 3000 rpm for 30 s. Finally, 100 nm Au film, as a counter electrode, was deposited via thermal evaporation using a shadow mask.

The microstructure of the as prepared FGQDs were characterized by TEM and AFM. Fig. 1 a shows the homogeneous distribution of FGQDs, which mainly range in diameter from 1 nm to 4 nm. The average particle size is calculated to be 2.18 nm (Fig. 1b). The HRTEM image of FGQDs shows a lattice fringe with interplanar distance of 2.1 Å (inset in Fig. 1a), which can be ascribed to the (102) plane of FG crystal [23]. The diameter of FGQDs can be further acquired from the height profiles of atomic force microscope (AFM) image (Fig. 1c). AFM analysis indicates that the diameter of the selected FGQDs ranges from 1 nm to 2 nm (Fig. 1d), in accordance with TEM image.

X-ray photoelectron spectroscopy (XPS) and Raman spectroscopy were used to characterize the composition of FGQDs. As shown in Fig. 2 a, both FGQDs and FG have a distinct characteristic peak at 686 eV, which can be assigned to F 1s signal [24]. Moreover, the Raman spectrum of FGQDs shows distinct G peaks (1600 cm−1) and D peaks (1350 cm−1) [25], identical to that of FG crystal, Fig. 2b. The above findings indicate the structural integrity of FGQDs.

Fig. 3 a and b shows the SEM images of Cs0.05MA0.16FA0.79Pb(I0.83Br0.17)3 perovskite films without and with FGQDs, respectively. It can be seen that the average grain size (372.4 nm) of the pristine perovskite film is smaller than that of FGQDs-treated one (461.8 nm), Fig. 3c and d. Hence, it is reasonable to assume that F element plays an active role in regulating the growth of perovskite crystalline grains [26]. As observed from in-situ PL spectra of wet perovskites film, the addition of F atoms slows down the perovskite crystallization kinetics, and thus facilitates the growth of the perovskite grains, Fig. 3e and f. As shown in Fig. S1, the light absorption intensity of the perovskite film increased significantly after FGQDs treatment, which can be ascribed to the improved perovskite crystalline quality [27].

High-resolution X-ray photoelectron spectroscopy (XPS) analysis provided evidence of molecular interactions between PGQDs and perovskite. Fig. 4 a shows the Pb 4f XPS spectra of Cs0.05MA0.16FA0.79Pb(I0.83Br0.17)3 perovskite films. The pristine perovskite exhibits two main peaks of Pb 4f7/2 and Pb 4f5/2 at 138.4 and 143.2 eV, respectively [28]. Additional small peaks located at 136.5 and 141.3eV are corresponding to metallic Pb (Pb0), which arise from the decomposition of perovskite film [29]. In contrast, the binding energy of Pb 4f7/2 and Pb 4f5/2 in FGQD-modified perovskite sample was shifted to a lower value, respectively. Moreover, Pb0 characteristic peaks disappeared simultaneously. These findings can be attributed to Lewis acid-base interaction of F atoms and undercoordinated Pb ions, which provides a higher perovskite crystallization possibility for the primary perovskite phase [30], in accordance with the SEM observation. Fig. 4b shows F 1s core-level XPS spectra, in which F 1s peak of FGQD-treated perovskite film (FGQD + PVK) shifts to a higher binding energy region compared to the pure FGQDs, further confirming the F–Pb coordination interaction.

As shown in Fig. 5 a, steady-state photoluminescence (SSPL) was used to study the carrier dynamics of Cs0.05MA0.16FA0.79Pb(I0.83Br0.17)3 perovskite films in the presence of HTL. As compared with the pristine film, the FGQDs-treated one showed the relatively strong PL quenching, which suggests more efficient hole transfer from the FGQD-treated perovskite film to HTL. To gain more insights into effective hole transfer dynamics, time-resolved PL (TRPL) measurements were carried out. As shown in Fig. 5b, FGQD accelerated the PL decay and shorted the lifetime from 4.48 μs to 1.29 μs. Hence, treating perovskite layer with FGQDs promoted charge extraction and inhibited carrier recombination [31]. Furthermore, the Kelvin probe force microscopy (KPFM) was employed to measure the surface potential of perovskite films. As shown in Fig. S2, FGQD treatment increases the surface potential of the perovskite film, leading to more p-type characteristics of the perovskite surface, indicative of an increased work function [32]. Meanwhile, electrochemical impedance spectroscopy (EIS) further provides insight into charge transfer behavior. Fig. 5c shows the Nyquist diagram with two typical arcs, in which one in the high frequency region was generally dominated by the charge transfer resistance (R tr) and the other in the low frequency region was associated with the recombination resistance (R rec) [33,34]. As can be seen from the table in the inset of Fig. 5b, the FGQDs-modified device presents a larger Rrec and a lower Rtr than the pristine device without FGQDs, indicating the improved charge transfer process, which is mainly attributed to the defect passivation via F–Pb coordination.

The optimized Cs0.05MA0.16FA0.79Pb(I0.83Br0.17)3 perovskites and corresponding PSCs without and with FGQDs treatment was labeled as control and target, respectively. Compared with the control PSCs (19.63 %), the champion target device yields a high PCE of 21.74 % with a short-circuit current (J
sc ) of 24.53 mA cm−2, an open-circuit voltage (V
oc ) of 1.15 V, and a fill factor (FF) of 77.07 % under AM 1.5G illumination [35], Fig. 6 a and Table S1. F–Pb coordination improves the crystalline quality of perovskite films, contributing to a stronger capacity of light absorption and hence a higher J
sc value. Meanwhile, F–Pb coordination also boosts the carrier transfer and reduces non-radiative recombination, resulting in higher V
oc and FF values. By comparing the photovoltaic performance under forward and reverse scan directions (Fig. 6a–Table S1), the target devices exhibit virtually negligible hysteresis [36]. Furthermore, when Cs0.05(FA0.98MA0.02)0.95Pb(I0.98Br0.02)3 perovskite, namely FA-based perovskite, is used as an active absorption layer, the corresponding device efficiency is increased to 24.12 %, indicating a strong universal function of FGQDs in organic–inorganic hybrid perovskite films. As shown in Fig. S3, statistical analyses of the photovoltaic parameters from 15 devices for each type of device reveals good repeatability for the FGQD-treated devices. To further verify the reliability of devices. Fig. 5c shows the steady-state output of the photocurrent versus time for the control and target devices measured at the maximum power point (MPP). The target device yields a stable J
sc of 22.13 mA cm−2 and a stable PCE of 20.80 % for 180 s at a bias voltage of 0.94 V. In contrast, the control device delivers a low PCE of 17.62 % at a bias voltage of 0.82 V. The long-term humidity stability of devices is crucial for the commercial application. After the unencapsulated devices were stored in humid environment (RH = ∼50 %) in the dark for 30 days, the PCE of the control device decreases to less than 40 %, while that of the target device remains above 85 %, Fig. 6d. The significantly improved stability can be attributed to the excellent hydrophobic performance of F atoms, which can be supported by water contact angle test, as seen in the insets of Fig. 6d. FGQDs at perovskite GBs can efficiently prevent the moisture from permeating into grain interiors, thereby inhibiting the water-associated degradation of the perovskite film.

In summary, as an additive of antisolvent, FGQDs endow the perovskite layer with excellent resistance to degradation in high-humidity environment. Meanwhile, the defects in the perovskite films were significantly eliminated via passivation strategy, thereby improving carrier extraction and suppressing charge recombination. The target device achieved a higher PCE (21.74 %) than the control one (19.63 %). A higher PCE of 24.12 % is delivered for the FA-based device. After 30 days of exposure in a dark humid environment (50 % RH) at room temperature, the efficiency of the target device remained above 85 % of its initial efficiency, indicating the excellent long-term humidity stability. This work provides an effective passivation strategy for efficient and stable PSCs.

Yao Chen: Writing – original draft, Methodology, Formal analysis. Jiajun Chen: Formal analysis. Xiaorui Wang: Formal analysis. Penglin Deng: Visualization. Yijun Shen: Writing – review & editing, Supervision, Funding acquisition. Xiaohong Wang: Writing – review & editing, Supervision, Funding acquisition.
The data that support the findings of this study are available from the corresponding author upon reasonable request.

This work was supported by the National Natural Science Foundation of China (22365013), 2022 Natural Science Cultivation Project for Young Teachers in Hainan University (Grant No. QNPY-2202-17) and the Hainan Provincial Postgraduate Innovative Research Project (Grant No. Qhyb2022-87).

ce:conflict-of-interest.ce:para: The authors declare that they have no known competing financial interests or personal relationships that could have appeared to influence the work reported in this paper.

sb:title.sb:maintitle: Recent progress of critical interface engineering for highly efficient and stable perovskite solar cells

Rational design of Lewis base molecules for stable and efficient inverted perovskite solar cells. Science 2023;79:690-694.
Kim JY, Lee JW, Jung HS, Shin H, Park NG. High-efficiency perovskite solar cells. Chem Rev 2020;120:7867-7918.

sb:title.sb:maintitle: Organometal halide perovskites as visible-light sensitizers for photovoltaic cells

sb:title.sb:maintitle: Surface passivation of perovskite film for efficient solar cells

sb:title.sb:maintitle: Zinc oxide sensitized graphene quantum do “ZnO-GQDs”: a hybrid concept to study charge transfer and its catalytic applicability to synthesize tetrasubstituted propargylamines
